prompt:
`tempjanitor.sh` is our small cleanup utility for a per-job temporary directory. A recent safety review found that it can reclaim a directory while a process still has a file open below that directory. Repair that defect without weakening any of the existing safeguards or changing the command's interface or audit schema.

Invocation is `bash tempjanitor.sh [--dry-run] --root DIR --age SECONDS --owner UID --lock FILE --now EPOCH`. All value options are required, numeric values are unsigned decimal integers, unknown/missing arguments print the usage line to stderr and exit 64, and the root must be a real directory rather than `/` or a symlink. `--now` is deliberately explicit so scheduled runs and tests make the same age decision: an immediate child is old enough when its own mtime is at or before `EPOCH - SECONDS`.

The janitor considers only immediate children of DIR. A candidate may be reclaimed only when it and every entry below it are owned by UID and are on the same device as DIR. A top-level symlink is skipped, and directory walks must never follow nested symlinks or cross a filesystem boundary. If any process has the candidate itself, or any path below a candidate directory, open through `/proc/*/fd`, the entire candidate is skipped. The lock is a nonblocking `flock(1)` held for the complete scan; contention produces no audit records or candidate changes, prints `tempjanitor.sh: lock busy: FILE`, and exits 75.

Successful runs write deterministic JSON Lines to stdout: one candidate record per immediate child in byte-sorted path order, then one summary. Skip reasons are `symlink`, `filesystem`, `owner`, `young`, or `open`; eligible dry-run records use action `would_remove`, live records use `removed`, and eligible records include the sum of regular-file content bytes in that candidate without following links or crossing devices. Dry-run may create/open the lock file but cannot change the candidate tree. A second identical live run must be idempotent. Keep stderr empty on successful scans.

The protected harness uses fixed mtimes and a local fake `stat` boundary to exercise the same-device and ownership decisions without mounts or privileges. It also holds old top-level files and a descendant file open using real descriptors, including path-component boundary, trailing-newline, and literal ` (deleted)` suffix cases, verifies exact reclaimed bytes and tree contents, checks lock contention, dry-run behavior, symlink safety, byte-exact JSONL, and a no-op second run. Run `bash test_tempjanitor.sh`; do not modify the test.
